content:
The degradation of encapsulant-substrate adhesion under accelerated environmental stress testing constitutes a central reliability concern for flexible electronic modules. Among the principal encapsulant families deployed in photovoltaic and flexible electronics applications—including ethylene vinyl acetate (EVA), polyolefin elastomers (POE), thermoplastic polyolefins (TPO), polyetherimides (PEI), and ionomer-based systems such as Surlyn—cross-linking encapsulants have received the most systematic investigation under damp heat conditions. The standard accelerated aging protocol, defined by IEC 61215 and IEC 61646, prescribes continuous exposure at 85°C and 85% relative humidity (RH) for durations ranging from 1,000 to 3,000 hours, establishing the benchmark environmental dose for qualifying module-level adhesion durability.

Interfacial adhesion performance under damp heat aging is conventionally assessed through peel testing at the glass-encapsulant interface, complemented by gel content measurements and yellowness index tracking as secondary degradation indicators [REF:2]. The gel content metric serves as a proxy for cross-link density in thermosetting encapsulants, while the yellowness index captures oxidative and hydrolytic degradation of the polymer matrix. These complementary indicators enable differentiation between bulk polymer degradation and interface-specific failure mechanisms—a distinction of considerable practical importance, given that thermal degradation of the interface can be significant even when the bulk encapsulant exhibits negligible degradation in the absence of UV irradiation and elevated humidity [REF:1].

The lamination process parameters exert a strong influence on initial and post-aging adhesion strength. For cross-linking encapsulants such as EVA and POE, higher lamination temperature was beneficial for both peel force and gel content before and after damp heat aging [REF:2]. This finding indicates that the degree of cross-linking achieved during lamination directly governs the resistance of the encapsulant-glass interface to hydrolytic and thermally driven degradation. The convergence of improved initial peel force with superior post-aging retention at elevated lamination temperatures suggests that under-cured laminates may be disproportionately vulnerable to moisture-induced delamination [REF:2][REF:1].

Despite these advances for EVA and POE systems, significant evidence gaps persist regarding the behavior of DVS-functionalized polyetherimide (DVS-PEI) encapsulants under equivalent damp heat protocols. The existing literature has not yet established quantitative peel force, gel content, or yellowness index baselines for DVS-PEI systems subjected to IEC 61215/61646 testing, nor has the lamination-temperature dependence of adhesion retention been characterized for this material class. The mechanistic pathways by which moisture ingress at 85°C/85%RH may disrupt DVS cross-links or the PEI backbone remain underexplored. Furthermore, whether the interface-specific degradation phenomenon documented for conventional cross-linking encapsulants [REF:1] extends to the fundamentally different chemistry of DVS-PEI systems remains an open question requiring targeted experimental investigation.

**Key Findings**

- Thermal degradation at the encapsulant-substrate interface can proceed independently of bulk polymer degradation, particularly in the absence of UV and elevated humidity [REF:1].
- Higher lamination temperatures improved both initial peel force and post-aging adhesion retention for cross-linking encapsulants (EVA and POE) under damp heat conditions up to 3,000 hours [REF:2].
- Adhesion characterization under damp heat relies on peel testing, gel content, and yellowness index as complementary degradation indicators [REF:2].
- Quantitative adhesion data for DVS-PEI encapsulants under IEC 61215/61646 damp heat protocols are absent from the current evidence base, representing a critical gap for flexible electronics reliability assessment.
